lines.greek: ὣς ἔφαθʼ, οἱ δʼ ἄρα πάντες ὀδὰξ ἐν χείλεσι φύντες
Τηλέμαχον θαύμαζον, ὃ θαρσαλέως ἀγόρευεν.
τὸν δʼ αὖτʼ Ἀντίνοος προσέφη, Εὐπείθεος υἱός·
Τηλέμαχʼ, ἦ μάλα δή σε διδάσκουσιν θεοὶ αὐτοὶ
ὑψαγόρην τʼ ἔμεναι καὶ θαρσαλέως ἀγορεύειν·
μὴ σέ γʼ ἐν ἀμφιάλῳ Ἰθάκῃ βασιλῆα Κρονίων
ποιήσειεν, ὅ τοι γενεῇ πατρώιόν ἐστιν.
τὸν δʼ αὖ Τηλέμαχος πεπνυμένος ἀντίον ηὔδα·
Ἀντίνοʼ, ἦ καί μοι νεμεσήσεαι ὅττι κεν εἴπω;
καί κεν τοῦτʼ ἐθέλοιμι Διός γε διδόντος ἀρέσθαι.
ἦ φῂς τοῦτο κάκιστον ἐν ἀνθρώποισι τετύχθαι;
οὐ μὲν γάρ τι κακὸν βασιλευέμεν· αἶψά τέ οἱ δῶ
ἀφνειὸν πέλεται καὶ τιμηέστερος αὐτός.
ἀλλʼ ἦ τοι βασιλῆες Ἀχαιῶν εἰσὶ καὶ ἄλλοι
πολλοὶ ἐν ἀμφιάλῳ Ἰθάκῃ, νέοι ἠδὲ παλαιοί,
τῶν κέν τις τόδʼ ἔχῃσιν, ἐπεὶ θάνε δῖος Ὀδυσσεύς·
αὐτὰρ ἐγὼν οἴκοιο ἄναξ ἔσομʼ ἡμετέροιο
καὶ δμώων, οὕς μοι ληίσσατο δῖος Ὀδυσσεύς.
τὸν δʼ αὖτʼ Εὐρύμαχος Πολύβου πάϊς ἀντίον ηὔδα·
Τηλέμαχʼ, ἦ τοι ταῦτα θεῶν ἐν γούνασι κεῖται,

interlinear: so he-spoke; and-they all, with-teeth in their-lips fastening,
at-Telemachus marvelled, that boldly he-spoke.
and-him in-turn Antinous addressed, of-Eupeithes son:
'Telemachus, surely indeed you the-gods themselves are-teaching
a-lofty-speaker to-be, and boldly to-speak.
may-not you in sea-encircled Ithaca king the-son-of-Cronos
make, which for-you by-birth from-your-father is.'
and-him in-turn Telemachus, prudent, answering addressed:
'Antinous, will-you indeed be-angry-at-me for-what I-shall-say?
even this I-would-wish, Zeus at-least granting, to-win.
do-you-say this worst among men to-have-been-made?
for-not at-all an-evil-thing is-it to-be-king; and-quickly his house
wealthy becomes, and more-honoured he-himself.
but truly indeed kings of-the-Achaeans there-are also others,
many, in sea-encircled Ithaca, young and old,
of-whom let-someone this hold, since died goodly Odysseus.
but I of-our-house lord shall-be, of-ours,
and of-the-slaves, whom for-me won goodly Odysseus.'
and-him in-turn Eurymachus, of-Polybus son, answering addressed:
'Telemachus, truly indeed these-things on-the-gods' knees lie,
literal: So he spoke; and they all bit their lips,
and marvelled at Telemachus, that he spoke so boldly.
Then Antinous, son of Eupeithes, answered him:
'Telemachus, surely the gods themselves are teaching you
to be a lofty speaker and to speak boldly.
May the son of Cronos never make you king
in sea-encircled Ithaca, though it is yours by birth.'
Then prudent Telemachus answered him:
'Antinous, will you be angry with me for what I shall say?
I would gladly take even this, if Zeus should grant it.
Do you say that this is the worst thing that befalls men?
It is no evil thing to be king; quickly his house
grows wealthy, and he himself is more honoured.
But truly there are other kings of the Achaeans also,
many, in sea-encircled Ithaca, both young and old;
let one of them hold this, since goodly Odysseus is dead.
But I will be lord of our own house,
and of the slaves that goodly Odysseus won for me.'
Then Eurymachus, son of Polybus, answered him:
'Telemachus, truly these things lie upon the knees of the gods,
translations.elizabethan:
So spoke he; and they every one of them
bit hard upon their lips, and marvelled at him,
that Telemachus should speak so bold.
Then Antinous, Eupeithes' son, replied:
"Telemachus, the gods themselves must teach thee
to talk so high and answer with such nerve.
Now never may the son of Cronos make thee
king in sea-encircled Ithaca,
although by birth the thing is thine to hold."
Then prudent Telemachus made answer:
"Antinous, wilt thou be angry at my word?
I'd take even that, if Zeus should hand it me.
Say'st thou it is the worst of fates for men?
It is no evil thing to be a king.
His house grows rich apace, and he himself
is held in higher honour. But indeed
there are Achaean kings enough besides
in sea-encircled Ithaca, both young
and old; let one of them possess the thing,
now goodly Odysseus is dead. But I
will be the master of our own house here,
and of the slaves he won for me, that man."
Then answered him Eurymachus, the son
of Polybus: "Telemachus, these things
lie surely on the knees of the gods."
translations.modernist:
That is what he said. They all bit their lips. It surprised them that Telemachus talked that way.

Then Antinous, the son of Eupeithes, answered him.

"Telemachus. The gods themselves must be teaching you. To talk big. To speak up like that. I hope the son of Cronos never makes you king in sea-encircled Ithaca, even if it is yours by birth."

Then Telemachus answered him. He had sense.

"Antinous. Will you be angry if I say something? I would take it, if Zeus handed it to me. Do you think that is the worst thing that can happen to a man? It is not a bad thing to be king. The house gets rich fast and the man himself is thought more of.

But there are other Achaean kings in sea-encircled Ithaca. Plenty of them, young and old. Let one of them have it, now that goodly Odysseus is dead. I will be master of our own house, and of the slaves he took for me."

Then Eurymachus, the son of Polybus, answered him.

"Telemachus. These things lie on the knees of the gods."
translations.storybook:
That was what he said, and every one of them bit his lip in surprise, for they had never heard Telemachus speak so boldly before.

Then Antinous, the son of Eupeithes, answered him.

"Telemachus! The gods themselves must be teaching you to make grand speeches and to answer people back. Well, I hope the son of Cronos never makes you king in sea-encircled Ithaca, even though it is yours by right of birth."

Then sensible young Telemachus answered him.

"Antinous, will you be cross with me if I say something? I would take the crown gladly, if Zeus handed it to me! Do you really think that is the worst thing that can happen to a person? It is no bad thing to be a king. His house grows rich very quickly, and everyone thinks more of him.

But there are plenty of other Achaean kings in sea-encircled Ithaca, young ones and old ones. Let one of them have it, now that my father is dead. I shall be master of my own house, and of the servants my father won for me."

Then Eurymachus, the son of Polybus, answered him.

"Telemachus, these things lie upon the knees of the gods."

murray.text: to be a man of vaunting tongue, and to speak with boldness. May the son of Cronos never make thee king in sea-girt Ithaca, which thing is by birth thy heritage.” Then wise Telemachus answered him: “Antinous, wilt thou be wroth with me for the word that I shall say?

Even this should I be glad to accept from the hand of Zeus. Thinkest thou indeed that this is the worst fate among men? Nay, it is no bad thing to be a king. Straightway one's house grows rich and oneself is held in greater honor. However, there are other kings of the Achaeans

full many in seagirt Ithaca, both young and old. One of these haply may have this place, since goodly Odysseus is dead. But I will be lord of our own house and of the slaves that goodly Odysseus won for me.” Then Eurymachus, son of Polybus, answered him:

“Telemachus, this matter verily lies on the knees of the gods, who of the Achaeans shall be king in sea-girt Ithaca; but as for thy possessions, thou mayest keep them thyself, and be lord in thine own house. Never may that man come who by violence and against thy will shall wrest thy possessions from thee, while men yet live in Ithaca.
